justification.Recall@K: Recall@10=1.0000. Recall@10 measures whether all queried tickers appear in top-10 results. This value is critical for financial Q&A. Missed ticker = missed facts.
justification.MRR: MRR=0.6000. MRR measures the rank position of the first relevant chunk. Low MRR buries relevant context below noise in the LLM prompt.
justification.source_coverage: source_coverage=0.6000. Fraction of expected KB sources (['concepts', 'macro', 'portfolio', 'strategies', 'tickers']) that appear in top-10 results. Low coverage means the intent router is over-relying on one KB.

retrieved_chunks.label: Calendar Rebalancing
retrieved_chunks.text: Calendar Rebalancing (rebalancing): WHAT: Rebalance back to target allocations at fixed time intervals monthly, quarterly, semi-annually, or annually regardless of how much drift has occurred. WHEN: Applied mechanically at the chosen interval. Annual rebalancing is most common for tax efficiency; quarterly for more active risk management. Monthly is generally over-trading for most portfolios. HOW: At the rebalancing date: (1) Calculate current portfolio weights. (2) Compare to target. (3) Sell overweight positions, buy underweight. (4) If using dividend income or new contributions, direct cash flow to underweight positions first before selling anything. WHY: Simple, predictable, and requires no continuous monitoring. Ensures portfolio risk doesn't drift for more than one period. Particularly suitable for tax-advantaged accounts (no capital gains concern) where simplicity of execution is valued. RISKS: Ignores the magnitude of drift you might rebalance when positions are only 1 percent off target (unnecessary trading costs) and miss rebalancing when they are 15 percent off target mid-period. Less responsive to sudden market events. Trigger conditions: Scheduled quarterly review date reached; Annual tax year end (for tax-loss harvesting alignment); New contributions or withdrawals of >5 percent of portfolio value. Related frameworks: threshold_rebalancing, tax_loss_harvesting.

retrieved_chunks.label: Maximum Drawdown (MDD)
retrieved_chunks.text: Maximum Drawdown (MDD): Maximum drawdown is the largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value over a given period, expressed as a percentage of the peak value. MDD = (Trough Value − Peak Value) / Peak Value. It measures the worst-case loss an investor would have experienced if they bought at the peak and sold at the trough. A 50 percent drawdown requires a 100 percent subsequent gain just to return to breakeven — the asymmetry makes MDD one of the most psychologically important risk metrics. Example: Portfolio grows from 100.00 thousand dollars to 150.00 thousand dollars (peak), then falls to 90.00 thousand dollars (trough). MDD is equal to (90.00 thousand dollars − 150.00 thousand dollars) divide by 150.00 thousand dollars which is equal to negative 40 percent. Note that this is a 40 percent loss from peak, not a 10 percent loss from the original 100.00 thousand dollars starting value. Risk relevance: MDD above 20 percent signals historically painful drawdowns. High-concentration tech portfolios have experienced 40-70 percent MDD in bear markets. Diversification and rebalancing are the primary tools to reduce MDD. Related concepts: var, volatility_21d, sharpe_ratio, rebalancing.

retrieved_chunks.label: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)
retrieved_chunks.text: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI): The HHI measures portfolio concentration as the sum of squared weights. It ranges from 1/N (perfectly equal-weight, maximum diversification) to 1.0 (single position, maximum concentration). HHI = Σ w_i². For a 5-asset equal-weight portfolio: HHI = 5 × (0.2²) = 0.20. For a single position: HHI = 1.0. A useful normalised version: (HHI − 1/N) / (1 − 1/N) maps the range to [0, 1] regardless of portfolio size. Originally used in antitrust economics to measure market concentration; adapted for portfolio analysis. Example: My portfolio has a share of AAPL of 50 percent, MSFT of 30 percent and NVDA of 20 percent. HHI = 0.5² + 0.3² + 0.2² is equal to 0.25 + 0.09 + 0.04 is equal to 0.38. Equal-weight 3-stock benchmark HHI is then equal to 0.33. The concentrated portfolio has HHI 0.38 vs benchmark 0.33 — moderately more concentrated than equal-weight. Risk relevance: HHI above 0.25 in a 10-asset portfolio signals meaningful concentration. HHI above 0.5 in any portfolio signals very high single-name risk. Rebalancing is the remedy — trim the largest weights to bring HHI down. Related concepts: concentration_risk, risk_contribution, pairwise_correlations, rebalancing.

retrieved_chunks.label: All-Weather Portfolio (Dalio)
retrieved_chunks.text: All-Weather Portfolio (Dalio) (asset_allocation): WHAT: Ray Dalio's All-Weather portfolio is designed to perform in all four economic environments: (1) rising growth, (2) falling growth, (3) rising inflation, (4) falling inflation. Allocation: 30 percent equities, 40 percent long-term bonds, 15 percent intermediate bonds, 7.5 percent gold, 7.5 percent commodities. WHEN: Designed as a permanent strategic allocation not a tactical strategy. Suitable for investors who cannot predict which macro environment comes next and want resilience across all scenarios. Particularly appropriate during periods of macro uncertainty. HOW: Buy and hold with annual rebalancing. Equity component: VTI or SPY. Bond components: TLT (long-term) and IEF (intermediate). Gold: GLD. Commodities: DJP or GSG. Rebalance annually back to target weights. WHY: Backtests show All-Weather delivered approximately 7 to 8 percent annual return with a maximum drawdown of approximately −12 percent (2020 COVID crash). It severely underperformed in 2022 (−21 percent) when both bonds and equities fell, but historically the diversification across gold and commodities provided partial protection. RISKS: Heavy bond allocation underperforms in rising rate environments. Returns are lower than pure equity in bull markets. The portfolio was designed for an era of lower inflation future performance with structurally higher inflation is uncertain. Trigger conditions: Investor cannot tolerate more than 15 to 20 percent drawdown; Macro environment is uncertain or transitioning between regimes; Investor has a very long time horizon (20+ years). Related frameworks: risk_parity, sixty_forty, threshold_rebalancing.
